author: @one-more-land
text: actually, as soon as you patent it and register it in their system, you sign it off to them, legally. That's why it's a bad idea. There are other ways of legally securing your own inventions, but it demands legal knowledge. Also, patents loose their validity usually around the 20 year mark.

author: @BaliFoodTreePlanter
text:
My friend who was the inventor for the Lockhead for stealth jet with 27 patents, taught me to not ever give my designs away in patents, but protect my great ideas in trade secrets.

This has allowed me to move appropriate tech forward 4 times, but not prevent BAD business from  its CALCULATED FAILURES AS NORMAL BUSINESS.

Therefore, I hold a few tools for these times, still.

What fun I had, saving the screws and looking up what a capacitor was and those wierd selenium rectifiers. Now, a boy has to ruin it to fix it.

author: @Amerikan.kartali.turk.yilani.
text: Super reasons. Thanks a million. I really appreciate it. Please keep it up. We should facilitate innovation as much as possible. Nobody can create from nothing. Innovators always utilize available knowledge and technology. Knowledge belongs to humanity. Basic education which is a pre-condition for any innovation, is paid by tax dollars. Most inventions at labs are facilitated by public tax dollars anyways. New innovations should not be blocked by patents. US has horrible patent system. It is losing innovation edge. China seems less caring about patents and so winning the innovation war. Instead of spending billions on patent protection, we should create an incentive system to encourage any citizen to innovate, produce and market products as much as possible. I suggest innovative crowdsourcing, crowdfunding, crowdmarketing.

author: @andrewchurchley5705
text: Hi, I have 20 years of translating and abstracting diverse scientific and engineering German patents on a daily basis and can only agree with your three major objections. I have felt sorrow for the private individuals who have paid good money to give away their ideas. I have no doubt many were aged and poor, without the least idea of how to exploit their provisional patents. They do not stand a chance against the big multinationals. When the big boys go to war over patent protection, the patent agents and patent courts are real winners.

Your ideas are sound, pure, and inspiring.

"Flash of Genius" was a good movie, but all I saw was a man consumed with a lust for recognition. It's not to say Ford was in the right, and certainly an individual should not be denied credit and compensation for a contribution, but I wonder how many more contributions he could have made to science or technology if he had spent all those years in a lab or teaching instead of obsessing over thousands of pages of legal documents.

author: @amirrahiminia2556
text:
I agree that patent establishment made more trouble than benefit and it works well only for rich lobbying minority.  We all know how important knowledge is to advance a society. Even if current patent service was working, by giving exclusive rights to someone, we have  somehow contributed to suppress the possible rate of development. We all pay increasing taxes and in most cases, increased costs for exclusively owned goods and services for the benefit of very few shrinking portion of a population. A suggestion is: Why don't we all pay for any new knowledge in advance to cover all research costs and acceptable profit for a worthy researcher like yourself Robert Murray-Smith?  That way information becomes free and the true form of it can be published to boost the life conditions for all and create a healthy competition to contribute to better accessibility of human needs. 
This is not something that the minority beneficiary powerful portion can possibly have any will to do it and it definitely needs movement from majority powerless to make it a reality.

author: @RedRake
text: I was working on a new rechargeable battery when I realized part of it was similar to a patented electroplating process. Could I still build my battery if it doesn't completely match any one of the claims? Also, this patent is from 60 years ago. Would it have expired? Do I need to search and read every single similar patent to make sure it's okay to build my battery?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Red Rake patents last 20 years mate and a new patent is supposedly granted for significant changes - there is no clear definition on what is significant though - so - basically - you have to wing it and hope the patent office agrees with you that the steps you take are -inventive' Actually - in practice - it doesn't matter what your patent application says - it will always come back non inventive - you will have to argue that it is. You can do a search if you like - but you can also pay the patent office to do that search for you - it's pretty cheap - around £150 i think

replies.author: @RedRake
replies.text: Oh, I wasn't going to patent my design. I was just wondering if it would be okay to make my battery if it's similar to the patent but isn't exactly the same.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Red Rake in research you can do whatever you like - to sell it you might have to think about that

replies.author: @RedRake
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Okay, thanks for the info.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Red Rake no worries mate - you know that

author: @programify...
text: Long ago I arrived at the same conclusion as you Robert. The patent system is indeed broken. It's infuriating to see dragons on Dragon's Den scold inventors because they don't have a patent; Has any dragon ever gone through the process of fighting a patent infringement against Apple, Microsoft, Google, IBM, etc. To prevent or at least impede copyists, I like to work on ideas which combine both software and electronics. Copying hardware (electronic/mechanical) components is usually relatively straight forward, but the software component is naturally inscrutable and hopefully would dissuade the casual copyist. Keep up the good work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @johnnyj2066
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith Hi robert. i am new to all this. just a quick question. i have a good e-commerce website, app idea that i want to  develop. i am not into programming. so i would pay a programming company to get it done. but i dont want them to steal my business idea,  the structure, layout, functionalities of my website . What should i do? i will apply for trademark first, i guess and then what? plz help. i am from uk but i want to get a US, EU protection for my idea. thanks.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +johny j Mate - I can't really advise you as any advice i would give would constitute a liability on my part. That being said any reputable company just wouldn't stoop that low. If you are worried try getting the old friends network to work on it - chances are you know someone with the skills. If you do use a company get them to sign a non disclosure non circumvention before you disclose then get a contract - that's about all you can do - but it is usually enough

replies.author: @programify...
replies.text: +johny j , What I have found is that companies will ask me to sign and Non Disclosure Agreement which has an additional non-compete clause. If I were stupid enough to attempt to do bad things, I'd leave myself wide open to legal action, the damages sought would remove any profit, and more. Hope this helps...

Worse, patents are not enforced at invention date based on your lab books, they are only enforced based on patent date.

author: @jonnied5767
text:
In principle I dont mind a patent when there is a real discovery made by one person that very few other people could have discovered however most patents I get to hear about seem blindingly obvious or something that in time someone else would have discovered given a few more years.

Example - an expired patent I really disagree with. The patent was simply to sandblasting steel before electroplating rather than after electro plating to achieve a certain aesthetic look. When I stumbled across this patent I was already doing precisely the same thing as a result of intuition and experience of plating and sandblasting my self styled solution was to go straight for the method that had been patented without ever consulting anyone on the matter - it was just as much my original idea as  his original idea except he had been born before me. I dont consider this knowledge "ownable" - its obvious and I think it always belonged to everyone who stopped to think the matter over - thankfully this patent is dead and buried.

The problem I have with patents is that many people with specific expertise have ideas for things they might explore at a future date, these ideas should not necessarily belong to the first person (or more likely corporation) who has the money to secure a patent. There are millions of fledgling ideas in the backs of peoples heads right now - why should they risk finding that what they considered was theirs is now only available to them if they pay royalties?

Science and technology are like the process of global exploration of centuries gone by, if one explorer had not discovered a new world country then another would surely have come along ten or fifty years later and discovered the same.  

It seems that people with a new idea which they do not wish to patent or do not have funding to patent are at risk of being usurped by those who do, the only line of defence it seems is to make the idea totally public so that no-one single person can own the idea.

We should be mindful of the huge amounts of money required to develop new drugs so perhaps key areas need special ring fencing but in general I wonder if we would all be better off if patents did not exist - would technology and development suddenly stop just because an idea could not be patented? Would car manufacturers stop trying to design a safer car or television manufacturers stop trying to produce a clearer picture?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't think technology and development would stop - to my mind people are naturally creative - several studies have been done - but the most infamous is the steam engine - by securing so many patent Bolton and Watt effectively held back the development of engines for 20 years - patents actually hold back development

These laws only protect mega corporations and my work is stolen left and right...I now no longer sell it and I never put it on the internet....one glass company had the friggin nerve to send me a.catalogue where they stole two of my designs!!!
